title: The matching polytope has exponential extension complexity
speaker: Thomas Rothvoss
abstract: A popular method in combinatorial optimization is to express polytopes \(P\), which may potentially have exponentially many facets, as solutions of linear programs that use few extra variables to reduce the number of constraints down to a polynomial. After two decades of standstill, recent years have brought amazing progress in showing lower bounds for the so called extension complexity. However, the central question in this field remained wide open: can the perfect matching polytope be written as an LP with polynomially many constraints?

proof_stages.label: Extension-complexity framework and matching-polytope target
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines linear extensions and extension complexity, surveys why lower bounds are significant, recalls Edmonds's odd-cut description of the complete-graph perfect matching polytope, and states the main theorem that its extension complexity is exponential.

proof_stages.label: Slack matrices and Yannakakis factorization theorem
proof_stages.summary: Slack matrices and nonnegative rank are introduced, and Yannakakis's theorem is outlined to reduce lower-bounding extension complexity to lower-bounding the nonnegative rank of a slack matrix.

proof_stages.label: Failure of ordinary rectangle-covering bounds
proof_stages.summary: The usual communication-complexity rectangle-covering lower bound is applied to the matching slack matrix but shown to be too weak because the positive entries are coverable by only polynomially many nonincident-edge rectangles, suggesting that large-slack entries are overcovered.

proof_stages.label: Hyperplane-separation witness and rectangle lemma
proof_stages.summary: A hyperplane-separation lower-bound method is introduced, a signed weight matrix is designed from the matching slack levels, and the proof is reduced to showing that every rectangle has exponentially small value under this linear functional.

proof_stages.label: Razborov-style partition sampling and entropy regularity
proof_stages.summary: Special partition rectangles for cuts and matchings are defined, the slack-level measures are re-expressed by random partitions, and an entropy/density lemma is introduced to justify conditioning on local events for most coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Good-partition comparison and slack-zero contradiction
proof_stages.summary: For good partition/triple pairs, the speaker compares the three-edge and k-edge measures and proves that two good triples must intersect in two edges, since otherwise a matching and cut in the rectangle would form a forbidden slack-zero entry.

proof_stages.label: Bad-partition error control and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The entropy regularity argument is used to bound the probability of bad matching and cut partitions, completing the rectangle lemma and hence the exponential nonnegative-rank and extension-complexity lower bound, followed by an SDP-extension open problem.

examples.note: The spanning-tree polytope is used to illustrate how exponentially many cut inequalities in an original formulation can sometimes be replaced by a polynomial-size extended formulation using auxiliary flow variables.
examples.engagement_reason: The spanning-tree polytope with flow variables is cited as an example where auxiliary variables give an n^3-size formulation, without constructing or analyzing it.
